title: The Art of Selective Neglect: How Top Students Prioritize Tasks
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have five assignments due this week. One is worth 20% of your grade. Two are worth 10% each. Two are worth 2% each. Which do you spend the most time on? The obvious answer is the 20% assignment. Yet many students spend hours perfecting the low-stakes busywork because it's easier. This is a trap. Top students practice selective neglect. Here is how they decide what to ignore.

The 80/20 Rule for Grades

In most classes, 20% of the assignments determine 80% of your grade. Find those assignments. They are usually the papers, the projects, the midterm, and the final. The daily homework? The low-stakes quizzes? The participation points? Those matter less.

Top students don't skip the low-stakes work entirely. They do enough to get full credit. Then they stop. They don't polish. They don't perfect. They move on to what actually matters.

If you spend 10 hours on a 2% assignment and 10 hours on a 20% assignment, you've made a terrible mistake. That 2% assignment should get 1 hour. The 20% assignment should get 15. The math is simple. The discipline is hard.

Know Your Grade Before You Start

Most students start the semester with no idea what they need to get an A. They try to get 100% on everything. That's exhausting and unnecessary.

Calculate: what grade do you want? What scores do you need on remaining assignments to get there? You might find that you can skip an assignment entirely or do a mediocre job and still reach your goal. That's not laziness. That's strategy.

Use a grade calculator app or spreadsheet. Update it weekly. Know exactly where you stand. The uncertainty of not knowing leads to anxiety and overworking. Clarity leads to efficiency.

The 'Good Enough' Threshold

For most assignments, there's a point of diminishing returns. The difference between a 90% and a 100% might take twice as much work. Is that work worth it? Sometimes yes. Often no.

Top students know where the threshold is for each class. In some courses, an 85% is an A. In others, you need a 93%. Adjust your effort accordingly. Don't fight against a grading scale that doesn't exist.

Urgent vs. Important (The Eisenhower Matrix)

Draw a square with four boxes:
• Urgent and important (do these first — studying for tomorrow's test)
• Not urgent but important (schedule these — a paper due next week)
• Urgent but not important (delegate if possible — a low-stakes online quiz)
• Not urgent and not important (eliminate — optional extra credit with tiny points)

Most students spend their time in the urgent quadrant, constantly firefighting. Top students spend time in the 'not urgent but important' quadrant. They work on the big paper before the panic sets in. They study for the final weeks in advance. They are proactive, not reactive.

Learn to Say No

Selective neglect isn't just about assignments. It's about opportunities. Club meetings. Social events. Extra volunteer shifts. Every yes is a no to something else. Top students say no to things that don't align with their goals.

This doesn't mean you should have no social life. It means you should be intentional. If you're behind in a class, say no to the optional party. If you're ahead, go. The decision is yours. Just make it consciously.

The Permission Slip

Give yourself permission to do a B- job on something low-stakes. Write the worst possible version of that discussion post. Turn in the problem set with minimal work. Skip the optional reading. The world will not end. Your GPA will not collapse.

In fact, doing poorly on low-stakes assignments can be liberating. You realize that perfection isn't required. You can prioritize. You can breathe.

Selective neglect is not about being lazy. It's about being strategic. Energy is finite. Hours are limited. Spend them where they matter most. The rest can wait — or be ignored entirely.
